attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to a neurology clinic with a six-month history of recurrent falls. He also reports a resting tremor in both hands and difficulty moving. His wife noticed he has difficulty closing his eyes, particularly when blinking.

His medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. His medications include verapamil, perindopril, atorvastatin, metformin, and empagliflozin. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history.

The neurologist suspects he has Parkinson's disease and so he is commenced on a trial of levodopa/carbidopa. The patient returns four weeks later. He has noticed no improvement in any of his symptoms.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This question is assessing knowledge of Parkinsonism and Parkinson-plus-syndromes, and highlighting the importance of red flag symptoms in a patient with Parkinsonism. Red flag symptoms in Parkinsonism include early onset falls, visual disturbances, and lack of response to levodopa treatment. These symptoms suggest a diagnosis other than Parkinson's disease, such as a Parkinson-plus syndrome.

Progressive supranuclear palsy is the most likely diagnosis. It is a Parkinson-plus syndrome characterised by early onset falls, visual disturbances, and dysphagia. The lack of response to levodopa is a key differentiator from Parkinson's disease. The difficulty with eye movements, particularly in blinking, is indicative of the supranuclear gaze palsy seen in this condition. Risk factors include age and possibly genetic predispositions, although the exact cause is unknown. Clinical findings often include axial rigidity, postural instability, and cognitive changes. Management is primarily supportive, focusing on symptom management, as there is no cure.

Parkinson's disease is a neurodegenerative hypokinetic movement disorder, characterised by bradykinesia, rigidity, and a resting tremor. This patient's presentation is suspicious of Parkinson's Disease but often falls do not manifest until later stages of the disease. A levodopa medication trial is given to patients with suspected Parkinson's disease to confirm or refute the diagnosis (as the underlying issue is a loss of dopamine production). In Parkinson's disease, there is usually symptomatic improvement. In this patient, there is no resolution of symptoms, indicating another diagnosis.

Multiple system atrophy is another Parkinson-plus-syndrome which manifests as Parkinsonism, along with early autonomic dysfunction (e.g., postural hypotension, arrhythmias, gastrointestinal upset, incontinence) - which is not present in this patient. It can also manifest cerebellar features e.g., nystagmus, incoordination, and slurred speech.

Corticobasal degeneration is a Parkinson-plus-syndrome which is characterised by Parkinsonism, akinetic rigidity in a unilateral limb, and apraxia or alien limb phenomenon.

This patient's medical and smoking history put him at risk of atherosclerosis, as well as small vessel disease, both of which lead to ischaemia. Multiple ischaemic events in the blood vessels supplying the basal ganglia could lead to the development of vascular Parkinsonism. A levodopa medication trial can be effective in improving symptoms in patients with vascular Parkinson's disease (as the underlying issue remains loss of dopamine), but it is not as effective compared with Parkinson's disease.
learningPoints: Progressive supranuclear palsy manifests as Parkinsonism with early onset falls, visual disturbances, and dysphagia.
Progressive supranuclear palsy is characterised by poor response to levodopa treatment.
Management of progressive supranuclear palsy is primarily supportive, focusing on symptom management.
references: Herwig A, Agic A, Huppertz HJ, Klingebiel R, Zuhorn F, Schneider WX, Schäbitz WR, Rogalewski A. Differentiating Progressive Supranuclear Palsy and Parkinson's Disease With Head-Mounted Displays. Front Neurol. 2021
DeMaagd G, Philip A. Parkinson's Disease and Its Management: Part 1: Disease Entity, Risk Factors, Pathophysiology, Clinical Presentation, and Diagnosis. P T. 2015
Vertes AC, Beato MR, Sonne J, Khan Suheb MZ. Parkinson-plus Syndrome. StatPearls. 2022

National Institutes of Neurological Disorders and Stroke. Progressive Supranuclear Palsy (PSP). NIH. N.d. Available from: https://www.ninds.nih.gov/health-information/disorders/progressive-supranuclear-palsy-psp
